US.name: United States

shortTerm.note: Can change overnight if debt demand weakens or net-selling starts

vitals.label: Debt vs income
vitals.read: Debt held by the public near one year of national income; projected to keep climbing.

vitals.label: Debt service vs income
vitals.read: Net interest to the public — cash actually leaving the government today — costs about a quarter of on-budget revenue. Gross interest, including bonds credited to trust funds, runs higher still (see the panel below).

vitals.label: Rates vs inflation & growth
vitals.read: 10-year Treasury yield minus trailing-12-month CPI inflation — the real cost of borrowing Dalio watches.

vitals.label: Debt & service vs savings
vitals.read: Reserves including gold at market value are still thin relative to the debt — and most of the buffer is illiquid gold, not spendable FX.

panels.note: Very large debt with little liquid backing. Who holds it matters: foreign holders can leave faster than domestic ones. Two debt-service rows below: net is what's actually leaving the government in cash today; gross adds interest credited to trust funds as bonds — a real claim, but not yet a cash outflow.

rows.label: Debt vs on-budget revenue
rows.src: derived · FYGFGDQ188S x GDP / Treasury (on-budget receipts, TTM)

rows.label: Gross interest (incl. intragovernmental)
rows.src: derived · Treasury (gross incl. GAS, on-budget receipts)

panels.note: The cushion a country can draw on before it must default or print. Gold is a hard-asset hedge, not spendable liquidity in the same way FX reserves are — Dalio tracks both, so both are shown here rather than picking one.
Economy-wide leverage and the external balance — how dependent the country is on foreign financing.

Dalio's single biggest risk mitigator. Dollar dominance across trade, debt, equities and reserves is what holds overall risk down — and what the US is undermining. Only the reserve series has long public history (IMF COFER, from 1999).

govGauge.note: Built mostly from borrowing need — including the stress case where creditors won't roll the debt over. Being able to borrow in your own currency pulls the score down.

cbGauge.note: Income statement looks only mildly negative; the balance sheet is about as stretched as it has ever been — lots of unbacked money against tiny reserves. Reserve-currency and institutional strengths keep the total moderate.

redFlags: A new round of QE to push real interest rates down.
The central government gaining control over the central bank.

provenance.revenueDefinition: on-budget receipts (Treasury MTS total receipts minus OASI+DI trust fund receipts, the statutory off-budget definition, 2 U.S.C. 622(7)) — the ONE revenue denominator shared by all three revenue-denominated rows below (net interest, gross interest, debt/revenue). Chosen 2026-07 after extending the debt-service matrix to 3 numerators x 3 denominators; net interest / on-budget receipts landed at 23.1%, ~1pt from Dalio's Ch.17 22% — see STATUS.md §9.
provenance.debtServiceBasis: NET interest to the public (excl. intragovernmental GAS) / on-budget receipts — headline `debt_service_to_revenue`. Revised 2026-07 from the prior gross/total-receipts basis: net-to-public is the numerator whose scope actually matches debt_to_gdp (debt held by the public); gross interest incl. GAS ships as its own separate row instead. See STATUS.md §9.
provenance.grossDebtServiceBasis: GROSS interest (incl. intragovernmental Government Account Series) / on-budget receipts — the explicit second debt-service row, same denominator as the net headline (see revenueDefinition).
provenance.debtToRevenueBasis: Debt held by the public ($, FYGFGDQ188S x GDP) / on-budget receipts (TTM, $) — same denominator as both debt-service rows. Dalio's Ch.17 table reports only debt/GDP, not debt/revenue, so there's no book figure to calibrate this row against; debt_to_gdp remains the headline.
provenance.reservesBasis: FRED reserves excl. gold + US gold holdings (Treasury, troy oz) x live gold price (DBnomics: IMF PCPS, indicator PGOLD — DBnomics' LBMA mirror was dropped 2026-07 after LBMA moved its price tables behind a members-only portal), gold valued at MARKET not the $42.2222/oz statutory book rate — resolved 2026-07 to match Dalio's Ch.17 US snapshot (3%); see STATUS.md
